metadata.title: Why Electric-Dipole Spontaneous Emission Speeds Up as Frequency Cubed

skin_warnings: B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Hydrogen's two-p state emits an ultraviolet photon and falls to one-s in about one point six nanoseconds. Lower the transition frequency while holding its electric-dipole strength fixed, and spontaneous emission slows dramatically.

props.command: Why does a free-space electric-dipole emission rate scale as frequency cubed?
props.runningText: counting photon modes and field amplitude…
output: Photon modes contribute two powers of frequency.
One-photon electric-field amplitude contributes one more.

beats.narration_text: The vacuum is the same vacuum at radio and optical frequencies. So why does the rate change so steeply? The answer has two factors: how many photon modes are available, and how strongly one photon drives an electric dipole.
Fermi's golden rule says rate equals a constant times the coupling matrix element squared times the density of final photon states. We only need the frequency scaling of those two pieces.

In three-dimensional free space, photon wave vectors fill spherical shells. A shell's area grows like k squared. Since k equals omega over c, the number of modes per frequency grows like omega squared.

Now normalize a single photon in a fixed volume. Its electric-field amplitude scales like the square root of omega. Squaring the electric-dipole coupling therefore supplies one additional power of omega.

Multiply the pieces: omega squared from photon phase space, times omega from the squared field amplitude. For an allowed electric-dipole transition in free space, A equals omega cubed times the dipole magnitude squared, with universal constants in front.

Hold the dipole matrix element fixed. Double the frequency and the rate grows by eight. Multiply frequency by ten and the rate grows by one thousand. Lifetime moves the other way because tau equals one over A.

A clean comparison uses two hypothetical electric-dipole transitions with the same dipole magnitude. Moving from three hundred megahertz to six hundred terahertz raises frequency by two million, so the omega-cubed factor alone raises the rate by eight times ten to the eighteenth.

Real transitions do not hold the matrix element fixed. Selection rules, wave-function overlap, and multipole type can suppress or enhance the coupling. Frequency cubed is only one factor in the full rate.

A proton spin flip is not an electric-dipole transition. It couples mainly through a magnetic dipole, with a nuclear magnetic moment, and an observed NMR relaxation time also depends on fluctuating fields and the sample environment. Do not compare it using the E1 formula alone.

Free space matters too. Put the emitter in a cavity, near a surface, or inside a photonic band gap, and the local density of electromagnetic states changes. The simple free-space omega-cubed law can be enhanced or suppressed.

The formula also assumes the electric-dipole and long-wavelength approximations. Forbidden E1 lines may decay through magnetic dipole, electric quadrupole, or multi-photon channels, usually with different scalings and much longer lifetimes.

Your turn. Two allowed electric-dipole transitions have the same dipole magnitude in free space. One has three times the angular frequency of the other. What is the ratio of their spontaneous-emission rates?
Three cubed is twenty-seven. The higher-frequency transition emits twenty-seven times faster, provided the dipole strengths and electromagnetic environment truly match.
Why Electric-Dipole Spontaneous Emission Speeds Up as Frequency Cubed.
